title: Ultrafast formation of a large dynamic magnetic soliton
authors: Ondřej Wojewoda, Sina Mayr, Miela J. Gross, Jan Klíma, Jaganandha Panda, Jakub Krčma, Jakub Holobrádek, Kristýna Davídková, Andrii V. Chumak, Igor Gerasimchuk, Roman Verba, Philipp Pirro, Markus Weigand, Simone Finizio, Morris Lindner, Carsten Dubs, Qi Wang, Sebastian Wintz, Caroline A. Ross, Michal Urbánek
abstract: Nonlinear magnetization dynamics offers a rich variety of phenomena ranging from bistability to chaos. Here, we report the ultrafast formation of a dynamic magnetic soliton in thin ferrimagnetic garnet films with perpendicular magnetic anisotropy, driven by the microwave magnetic field of a microstrip antenna. Using time-resolved Brillouin light scattering microscopy and scanning transmission X-ray microscopy, we directly track the build-up of the large-angle precession state. The observed soliton is distinct from other nonlinear magnetic excitations in two key aspects: (i) it forms inside the linear spin-wave frequency band, and (ii) it is exceptionally large, reaching tens of microns beyond the antenna. We explain the soliton formation by the self-limiting mechanism upon a positive nonlinear frequency shift and the spatial extent of the near-field of the antenna. At large distances from the drive, the soliton collapses and emits short-wavelength spin waves via almost instantaneous spatial wavenumber conversion. Time-resolved measurements further reveal a small finite delay during soliton formation, while coherent long-range oscillations appear essentially simultaneously over distances up to 40 micrometers. These results establish microwave-driven solitons as a robust nonlinear phenomenon in thin-film garnets and suggest opportunities for fast, nonlocal manipulation of magnetic states and for applications in novel computational schemes.
summary: This work demonstrates the ultrafast creation of large dynamic magnetic solitons in garnet films using microwave excitation. By combining advanced microscopy techniques, the authors characterize the soliton's unique formation mechanism and its subsequent collapse into propagating spin waves. This establishes a controllable, nonlinear method for manipulating magnetic states in thin films.
main_problem: The paper reports on the ultrafast formation and dynamics of large dynamic magnetic solitons in thin ferrimagnetic garnet films.
main_result: They established that microwave-driven solitons are a robust nonlinear phenomenon, characterized by formation inside the linear spin-wave band and subsequent emission of short-wavelength spin waves upon collapse.
method: The research combines time-resolved Brillouin light scattering microscopy ($\mu$-BLS) and scanning transmission X-ray microscopy (STXM) to track magnetization dynamics driven by a microstrip antenna.
model_or_system: The system involves thin ferrimagnetic garnet films (Bi:YIG and Ga:YIG) exhibiting perpendicular magnetic anisotropy. The dynamics are governed by nonlinear magnetization equations, which are analyzed against linear spin-wave theory.
key_observables: Precession angle ($\varphi$), nonlinear frequency shift, spatial decay of magnetization, and emitted short-wavelength spin waves.
important_parameters: The soliton exhibits an exceptionally large spatial extent (tens of microns) and forms much faster than linear spin-wave propagation.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the soliton is a dissipative excitation requiring continuous pumping, and that the transition from nonlinear to linear dynamics is rapid.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the experimental geometry, the nonlinear shift of FMR vs. precession angle, spatial decay of the precession angle, and time-resolved measurements showing oscillations and onset delays.
paper_structure: The paper systematically presents experimental characterization of the system, detailing the observation of the soliton, explaining its formation via a self-limiting mechanism, and finally demonstrating its collapse and subsequent spin-wave emission.
why_interesting: The study of nonlinear wave phenomena, such as solitons, in magnetic systems provides a physical realization of nonlinear wave dynamics, which has parallels in nonlinear optics and other areas of AMO physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1 Schematic picture of the soliton formation and spin-wave emission. a Experimental geometry. The exci- tation stripline is driven by an RF (radio frequency) generator with a single-frequency signal. The resulting magnetization dynamics (purple line) is subsequently measured by µ-BLS with intensity, time, and phase resolution or by time-resolved STXM. b Nonlinear shift of the ferromagnetic resonance as a function of the precession angle (φ). The inset illustrates an increase in the precession amplitude. The excitation frequency of 11 GHz is marked by the green line. Calculations use Bi:YIG material parameters (see Methods) and an in-plane external magnetic field of 550 mT. c Precession...

Fig. 2 Emission of spin waves at the soliton boundary in Ga:YIG. a, b Snapshots from time-resolved STXM (a) and simulation (b) at a fixed driving frequency of 3.57 GHz while sweeping the external magnetic field. c, d Nonlinear FMR shift at a precession angle of approx. 20◦(c) and the corresponding linear and nonlinear dispersion relations (d) for the Ga:YIG sample at an external field of 190 mT. The green dashed line shows the driving field frequency of 3.57 GHz. Dashed lines show the expected precession angle and wavenumber. e, Line profiles of the simulated intensity in (b) at 190 mT.

Fig. 3 Time-resolved observation of the soliton formation and long-range oscillations in Bi:YIG. a, Time traces of the square root of the BLS intensity recorded at different distances x from the antenna. Linear fits to the rising edges are depicted as faded solid lines. The inset highlights the distance-dependent onset delay of the soliton.b, Times of arrival extracted from the time to reach 80 % (red circles) and a linear fit used to determine the onset delay velocity in the nonlinear region (slope). In the linear region, the long-range oscillations follow the antenna excitation without a measurable delay. c, Slopes of the rising-edge fits from panel (a) (black circles). Blue squares...

Fig. 4 Frequency sweeps near and far from the antenna in Bi:YIG. a, b µ-BLS measurement of mag- netization dynamics at x = 1 µm (a) and x = 40 µm (b). The acquisition time for panel (b) was significantly longer. In panel (a) we used pulsed excitation to limit the heating effects. The color code is logarithmic. The signal with neg- ative slope is caused by the high signal and higher-order transmission through the interferometer.
